I think instead of trying the computational approach, if we use the alternative and more simplified expression for P_Alive which is Eq. 34 in the reference: http://brucehardie.com/notes/009/pareto_nbd_derivations_2005-11-05.pdf, this problem will be fixed.

I tried it , and it eliminates the overflow for high values of R,F, and T (verified for range of values >300).

below is the function with expression in 34. Note that parts of this equation as indeed present in conditional expected number of purchases.

```
def conditional_probability_alive_2(model, frequency, recency, T):
        params=model.params_.values()
        r,alpha,s,beta= params
        x, t_x = frequency, recency
        likelihood = -model._negative_log_likelihood(params, x, t_x, T,0.0) 
        first_term = gammaln(r + x) - gammaln(r) + r * log(alpha) + s * \
            log(beta) - (r + x) * log(alpha + T) - s * log(beta + T) 
        return exp(first_term - likelihood)
```

Kubernetes doesn't like annotations with null values when you create a new object (ConfigMap or Endpoint), therefore we are removing them here: https://github.com/zalando/patroni/pull/782/files#diff-87299faee0431ddce0848d6ff7726df7R264

But in case of PATCH request, null values have a different semantic. If a given annotation has null value in the PATCH request, Kubernetes supposed to remove such annotation from the object.

You are replacing null values with empty string, and as a result annotations are not removed:
```yaml
metadata:
  annotations:
    leader: ""
    member: ""
    scheduled_at: ""
```

From one side Patroni is able to handle it correctly (and acceptance tests prove that), but from another side, this is not how it supposed to work.

This is where I'm at for the docstring so far:

```
        r"""
        Internal implementation details (subject to rapid change): 
        Database entries go through several pipes, like so... 

        .. code:: 

                                                     -> hash-trie -> storage lookups
                                                    /
            db -----------------------> _journaldb ----------------> code lookups
             \
              > _trie -> _trie_cache -> _journaltrie --------------> account lookups

        Journaling sequesters writes here ^, until persist is called.

        _journaldb is a journaling of the keys and values used to store
        code and account storage.

        _trie is a hash-trie, used to generate the state root

        _trie_cache is a cache tied to the state root of the trie. It
        is important that this cache is checked *after* looking for
        the key in _journaltrie, because the cache is only invalidated
        after a state root change.

        _journaltrie is a journaling of the accounts (an address->rlp mapping,
        rather than the nodes stored by the trie). This enables
        a squashing of all account changes before pushing them into the trie.

        .. NOTE:: There is an opportunity to do something similar for storage

        AccountDB synchronizes the snapshot/revert/persist of both of the
        journals. 

        """
```

> Not understanding it gives way to 'magic' and allows concepts like 'sleeping' and 'waking' of the charm to come in.

I'm not convinced that this is true. Programming is all about creating good abstractions. If we can create a good abstraction on top of the hooks concept, we should do that. Stuff like files and folders are amazing abstractions. Most people use files every day without knowing how they actually work. Iptables is another example with the tables and chains. That's not how the code actually works, but the abstractions are so good that it doesn't matter to users of iptables.

Magic is when stuff happens that you can't explain using the abstractions. My comment further down elaborates a bit more on this, but the tldr is: "It's only safe to hide the hooks if we can explain all the behavior using the reactive concepts we present to the user."

"groups" are used in cuda-convnet for grouped convolution, where you learn kernels for subsets of the input channels. In this case, you don't specify the size of the pooling region/window/thing, but the number of groups, and if you use the same number for each convolutional layer, you get a multi-column architecture.
So this notion of "groups" is closely related to what we're doing here if axis=1, but as it doesn't have to be axis=1 and if we want to have a common term, I think "region" is a good choice. "window" is something I would associate with the last axis/axes in bc01* notation.

In depth citation, [per python 3 docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/email.header.html). (Note that the [python 2 counterpart is similar](https://docs.python.org/2/library/email.header.html)):

If you want to include non-ASCII characters in your email headers, say in the Subject or To fields, you should use the Header class and assign the field in the Message object to an instance of Header instead of using a string for the header value. Import the Header class from the email.header module. For example:

```
>>> from email.message import Message
>>> from email.header import Header
>>> msg = Message()
>>> h = Header('p\xf6stal', 'iso-8859-1')
>>> msg['Subject'] = h
>>> msg.as_string()
'Subject: =?iso-8859-1?q?p=F6stal?=\n\n'
```

Notice here how we wanted the Subject field to contain a non-ASCII character? We did this by creating a Header instance and passing in the character set that the byte string was encoded in. When the subsequent Message instance was flattened, the Subject field was properly RFC 2047 encoded. MIME-aware mail readers would show this header using the embedded ISO-8859-1 character.

pep8 allows for quite a bit of flexibility in formatting, and there are no really great code formatters for Python as in other languages, like Go and C. As a result, many different styles have emerged.

Please use the following layout for all method and function calls unless the situation strongly calls for something different. I know it's nit-picky of me to ask for this. But this layout is formulaic enough to work nearly everywhere, and using a consistent style makes it possible for people to easily scan through code.

```python
# Yes.
self.assertEqual(
    self.repo.last_response.json()['id'],
    self.__class__.__name__,
    set(self.repo.last_response.json())
)
self.assertEqual(self.repo.last_response.json()['id'], self.__class__.__name__)

# No: closing parenthesis is not at same indentation as start of statement.
self.assertEqual(
    self.repo.last_response.json()['id'],
    self.__class__.__name__,
    set(self.repo.last_response.json()))
# No: hanging arguments are used.
self.assertEqual(self.repo.last_response.json()['id'],
                 self.__class__.__name__,
                 set(self.repo.last_response.json()))
# No: multiple arguments on same line
self.assertEqual(
    self.repo.last_response.json()['id'], self.__class__.__name__
)
```

